target_tools.description:
Execute composable JavaScript to search Gmail, conversation memory, and Twitter in one call. Use return for output.
Available helpers: - search(query, opts?) → SearchResult[] — semantic/keyword/hybrid search over memory. Returns { sourceId, snippet, score, userName?, createdAt?, thread? }. opts: { mode?: "vector"|"keyword"|"hybrid", maxResults?, minScore? } - getMessages(opts?) → MessageResult[] — fetch messages by filter. Returns { id, userName, role, text, createdAt }. opts: { user?, after?, before?, role?, limit?, order? } - semanticFilter(msgs, concept, threshold?) → ScoredMessageResult[] — filter chat messages by meaning (pre-computed embeddings) - filterBySimilarity(items, concept, opts?) → {item, similarity}[] — generic semantic filter for any array - gmail.search(query, {max?}) → [{id, date, from, subject, labels, messageCount}] - gmail.messagesSearch(query, {max?, includeBody?}) → GmailMessage[] - gmail.get(messageId) → GmailMessage — { id, threadId, from, to, subject, date, body, labels, attachments } - gmail.thread(threadId, {full?}) → {id, messages} - twitter.feed(username?, page?) → XML timeline - twitter.search(query, page?, sort?) → XML results
Use Promise.all() for parallel searches. Keyword query syntax: "word word" (AND), "word OR word", "exact phrase", -word (exclude).

This isn't a "somewhere on the list" thing — this is the **most urgent item on the entire list.**

ABA providers typically have waitlists of **3-6 months**. If you're not calling Richmond-area providers this week, Jackson could go from 3x/week to nothing for a long stretch after you arrive. That gap is the single biggest risk of this move.

Here's what I'd do right now, in order:

1. **Call ABA providers in the Midlothian/Chesterfield area this week.** Get on multiple waitlists. Ask about intake timelines. Don't wait for the move — some may do virtual intakes before you arrive.
2. **Check husband's Capital One insurance for ABA and OT coverage.** Virginia has an autism insurance mandate, but plans vary. Know what you're working with before you pick providers.
3. **Request a full copy of Jackson's IEP and all evaluations** from his current school. Not just the summary — the full packet with testing data, progress notes, everything. You want this in hand before you leave.
4. **Contact Chesterfield County Public Schools special education office** — Midlothian falls under CCPS. Let them know you're coming, ask about their mid-year IEP transfer process. Legally they have to provide comparable services while they review his IEP, but "comparable" is a word districts interpret creatively. Having the full documentation makes that fight easier if it comes to one.

OT waitlists are usually shorter but still worth getting ahead of.

Yeah. That's the thing under all the logistics, isn't it.

And you're right to take it seriously — a cross-country move with new house, new school, new therapists, all of it at once? That's the biggest version of the thing that's hardest for him. Your worry makes sense.

But here's what's different from the apartment move: **you have weeks to prepare him, you have Miss Kelly, and you know it's coming.** That changes everything. Last time he was hit with change. This time you can build a bridge into it.

The biggest thing I'd do is **make this part of his therapy between now and January.** Tell Miss Kelly you want transition prep built into sessions — social stories about the move, what the new house looks like, what the drive will be like. She's been with him two years. She knows what his brain needs to make unfamiliar things feel safer.

A few things that tend to matter most:

**Before you go** — make Richmond real to him in small doses. Google Street View of the new house. "This is your room." Show him the neighborhood, the nearest park. Let him see it enough times that it's not brand new when he walks in.

**The drive itself** can actually work in your favor. Five days of gradual change is gentler than waking up in a new place one morning. Each day is a little different, but you and dad and the car are the same. It's a transition *into* transition.

**When you arrive** — his room gets set up first. Before the kitchen, before yours. His bed, his stuff, arranged as close to how it was as possible. That's his anchor point. Everything else can be chaos for a while as long he has one space that's *his*.

And — expect hard days. Don't aim for no meltdowns. Aim for *we have a plan for when they come, and we get through them together.* That's a more honest target, and it takes the pressure off all three of you.

You know him better than anyone. The fact that you're already thinking about this months out means he's got the right person in his corner.
